feecIe6418's blog

Editorial of Codeforces Global Round 21
By feecIe6418, 4 years ago,

Thanks for participation!

If your solution to D involves any data structures and is not O(n)O(n) -- please read the "solution 1". I believe it is very interesting, but to make the difficulty suitable for D we allowed not O(n)O(n) solutions.

1696A - NIT orz!

Hint 1

How many operations will we perform?

Answer

At most one. Why?

Solution

Suppose we can only perform exactly one operation. In this case the answer is S=max1≤i≤n(ai or z)S=max1≤i≤n(ai or z). In fact, we can prove that this is the answer.

Define a′iai′ as the value of aiai after some operations.

It suffices to prove the answer will never exceed SS. Note that zz will always become a submask of itself after any number of operations, so aiai will always be a submask of (ai or z)(ai or z) after any number of operations. This leads to the conclusion that a′i≤(ai or z)ai′≤(ai or z) for all ii. Thus max1≤i≤na′i≤max1≤i≤n(ai or z)=Smax1≤i≤nai′≤max1≤i≤n(ai or z)=S.

Time complexity is O(n)O(n).

1696B - NIT Destroys the Universe

Hint 1

How many operations will we perform?

Answer

At most two. Why?

Hint 2

How to check if the array can be destroyed in 00 or 11 operations?

Solution

The answer is at most 22, because doing the operation on [1,n][1,n] at most twice will always work. (If the array contains at least one zero, we need 22 operations. Otherwise we need 11 operation.)

If the array consists of zeroes, the answer is 00.

If all non-zero elements form a contiguous segment in the array, the answer is $1$​. To check this, you can find the leftmost and rightmost occurrence of non-zero elements and check if elements in the middle of them are also non-zero.

Otherwise the answer is 22.

Time complexity is O(n)O(n).

1696C - Fishingprince Plays With Array

Hint 1

The operation is reversible. (The two operations are reverses of each other.)

Hint 2

Try to find a middle state, such that we can turn both aa and bb into it.

Solution

Call the first operation "expand" and the second operation "shrink".

Keep doing expand on both arrays until we can't do expand anymore, call the resulting arrays a′a′ and b′b′. It suffices to check if a′=b′a′=b′. To implement this, you need to compress contiguous equal numbers.

Proof of why this is necessary and sufficient:

  • Sufficiency is obvious, since the operations are reversible. We can do something like a→a′=b′→ba→a′=b′→b.

  • Necessity: Let f(a)=a′f(a)=a′. It suffices to prove that an operation on aa does not affect f(a)f(a). An expand operation obviously doesn't affect f(a)f(a). A shrink operation shrinks a[i,i+m−1]a[i,i+m−1] into one element. When computing f(a′)f(a′), we will always expand a′iai′ at some time, so the result is the same as $f(a)$​.

Time complexity is O((n+k)logmV)O((n+k)logm⁡V), where V=maxaiV=maxai.

1696D - Permutation Graph

This problem has two different solutions. The first one is more beautiful, but less straight-forward.

Hint 1 for solution 1

The solution is O(n)O(n). We don't need any data structures.

Hint 2 for solution 1

Instead of trying to construct the shortest path from 11 to nn, find a "transfer vertex" that we must pass through.

Answer

We will always pass through the position of the maximum element in the array.

Hint 3 for solution 1

Suppose the maximum element is akak. Solve recursively for dis(1,k)dis(1,k) and dis(k,n)dis(k,n).

Solution 1

Denote dis(x,y)dis(x,y) as the length of the shortest path between xx and yy.

Consider a position ii that ai=nai=n. Assume i≠1i≠1 and i≠ni≠n. For a segment that passes ii, its maximum element is always aiai. Thus, for x<i<yx<i<y, xx and yy will never be directly connected by an edge. This means that when going from 11 to nn, we have to pass ii. Let us solve recursively for dis(1,i)dis(1,i) and dis(i,n)dis(i,n). For example, we solve for dis(1,i)dis(1,i).

We already know that ai=nai=n, so ii is the maximum element in [1,i][1,i]. Consider the minimum element in [1,i][1,i], suppose it is aj (j<i)aj (j<i). From similar arguments, we can solve recursively for dis(1,j)dis(1,j) and dis(j,i)dis(j,i). However, note that dis(j,i)dis(j,i) equals to 11: since jj and ii are respectively minimum and maximum in [1,i][1,i], they have to be minimum and maximum in [j,i][j,i] as well. So i,ji,j must be directly connected. Thus, we only need to solve recursively for dis(1,j)dis(1,j).

The process with dis(i,n)dis(i,n) is similar. Note that we will only call dis(l,r)dis(l,r) for l=1l=1 or r=nr=n (if not, the return value is always 1), so it suffices to pre-compute prefix and suffix minimums and maximums.

The time complexity is O(n)O(n).

Hint 1 for solution 2

Look at the last sample test case. Think of a simple greedy.

Answer

Keep going to the rightmost vertex (the vertex with the largest id) works.

Hint 2 for solution 2

Use data structures to simulate the process. How?

Solution 2

We can prove that keep going to the vertex with the largest index is a correct strategy. The proof is left as an exercise :) Hint: try to prove that the path we will visit is the same as the path we visited in solution 1.

Suppose we are at ii. We want to find the largest j>ij>i such that ii and jj are directly connected. WLOG, assume ai+1<aiai+1<ai. Then, it cannot be the case that aj>aiaj>ai, since none of ai,ajai,aj will be mn(i,j)mn(i,j). Thus aj<aiaj<ai. It follows that all i<k<ji<k<j satisfies ak<aiak<ai, otherwise none of ai,ajai,aj will be mx(i,j)mx(i,j).

Let riri be the largest pp, such that for all t∈[i+1,p]t∈[i+1,p], at<aiat<ai. From the arguments above we know j∈[i+1,ri]j∈[i+1,ri]. riri can be pre-computed with a stack, or binary search + some data structures.

Let j0j0 be the position of the minimum element in [i+1,ri][i+1,ri]. Obviously j0j0 is directly connected with ii. For any j0<k≤rij0<k≤ri, mn(i,k)mn(i,k) will be aj0aj0, showing that all such kk is not directly connected with ii. Thus, j0j0 is the desired jj.

If we use data structures for range minimum, we get a O(nlogn)O(nlog⁡n) solution, which can easily pass (not sure whether O(nlog2n)O(nlog2⁡n) ones will pass though, the large constraints were intended to cut those).

However, by building the cartesian tree of the array and doing proper pre-computations, we can optimize this solution to O(n)O(n).

1696E - Placing Jinas

Hint 1

Try to find out the number of operations we do on a specific cell (i,j)(i,j), call it f(i,j)f(i,j).

Hint 2

Write the recurrence formula for f(i,j)f(i,j). What is f(i,j)f(i,j)?

Answer

f(i,j)=(i+jj)f(i,j)=(i+jj)

Hint 3

The answer is the sum of f(i,j)f(i,j) over all white cells (i,j)(i,j). Use some combinatorics formula to speed it up.

Solution

Let us find out the number of operations we do on a specific cell (i,j)(i,j), call it f(i,j)f(i,j).

Every operation done on (i−1,j)(i−1,j) will lead to one doll on (i,j)(i,j), thus consuming one operation on (i,j)(i,j). Similar observation holds for (i,j−1)(i,j−1).

Thus, f(i,j)=f(i,j−1)+f(i−1,j)f(i,j)=f(i,j−1)+f(i−1,j) (given that (i,j),(i−1,j),(i,j−1)(i,j),(i−1,j),(i,j−1) are all white cells). Note that aa is non-increasing: this means that if (i,j)(i,j) is white, (i−1,j),(i,j−1)(i−1,j),(i,j−1) will both be white. So we can conclude that f(i,j)=f(i,j−1)+f(i−1,j)f(i,j)=f(i,j−1)+f(i−1,j) always holds as long as (i,j)(i,j) is white.

Another way to see the formula is f(i,j)f(i,j) is the number of ways to go from (0,0)(0,0) to (i,j)(i,j), only going down or right by 1 step. This implies that f(i,j)=(i+jj)f(i,j)=(i+jj).

From this, we know that the answer is ∑ni=0∑ai−1j=0(i+ji)∑i=0n∑j=0ai−1(i+ji). With the equation ∑ki=0(n+in)=(n+k+1n+1)∑i=0k(n+in)=(n+k+1n+1), we know that the answer is ∑ni=0(i+aii+1)∑i=0n(i+aii+1).

The time complexity is O(n+V)O(n+V), where V=maxaiV=maxai.

1696F - Tree Recovery

Hint 1

The solution does not contain painful casework and deadly implemention.

Hint 2

Suppose we aleady know edge (i,j)(i,j) exists in the tree. What can we know from it?

Answer

We can immediately recover the whole tree.

Solution

Read the hints first to understand the solution better.

Construct a graph with (n2)(n2) vertices (1,2),(1,3),…,(n−1,n)(1,2),(1,3),…,(n−1,n).

If dis(a,b)=dis(b,c)dis(a,b)=dis(b,c), link an undirected edge between (a,b)(a,b) and (b,c)(b,c).

Observe that all edges in the tree form a connected component of size exactly n−1n−1 in the graph!

Find all components of size n−1n−1 and try if all vertices in it form a tree that satisfy the input. There are at most n2n2 such components, so complexity is O(n4)O(n4). Proper pre-computation and the usage of bitsets can reduce the complexity to O(n4/w)O(n4/w).

1696G - Fishingprince Plays With Array Again

Hint 1

What kind of problem is this problem?

Answer

Linear programming.

Hint 2

Consider the dual.

Hint 3

Consider the case when n=2n=2. Draw the linear programming on a xOy-coordinate. Try to observe what the answer might be.

Solution

First we solve the problem with only 1 query on the whole array AA.

This is a linear programming problem:

minimize∑1≤i<nai+biXa1+Yb1≥A1Xai+Ybi+Yai−1+Xbi−1≥Ai (2≤i<n)Yan−1+Xbn−1≥Anai,bi≥0minimize∑1≤i<nai+biXa1+Yb1≥A1Xai+Ybi+Yai−1+Xbi−1≥Ai (2≤i<n)Yan−1+Xbn−1≥Anai,bi≥0

Consider its dual:

maximize∑1≤i≤nAixiXxi+Yxi+1≤1 (1≤x<n)Yxi+Xxi+1≤1 (1≤x<n)xi≥0maximize∑1≤i≤nAixiXxi+Yxi+1≤1 (1≤x<n)Yxi+Xxi+1≤1 (1≤x<n)xi≥0

Suppose X≤YX≤Y. Now we will prove that there exists an optimal solution to the dual problem, in which xixi can only take three values: 1/Y,1/(X+Y),01/Y,1/(X+Y),0.

The proof is as follows: It is well-known that an optimal solution to a linear programming problem must lie on a vertex of the "multi-dimensional convex polygon" which the restrictions surround. Thus we are only interested in xixi that satisfy several "=" restrictions (and the restrictions should really intersect at one point, meaning that those "=" should uniquely determine xx). Consider any "sufficient" (that means they uniquely determine xx) subset of them. If one restriction is related to xp,xqxp,xq, we link an undirected edge between pp and qq. If one restriction is only related to xpxp (i.e. xp=0xp=0), we link a self-loop on pp. "Being sufficient" means that all connected components in the graph has exactly one cycle. However, for an edge (u,v)(u,v), we know that either u=v+1u=v+1 or u=vu=v. This means that all cycles can only be (i→i+1→i)(i→i+1→i) or i→ii→i. If a cycle is (i→i+1→i)(i→i+1→i), all xixi in the component are 1/(X+Y)1/(X+Y); If a cycle is i→ii→i, all xixi in the component are 1/Y1/Y or 00 (not 1/X1/X, because it exceeds the constraints).

Thus we can use dp to solve the dual problem. Let dp(i,0/1/2)dp(i,0/1/2) be the maximum ∑j≤iAjxj∑j≤iAjxj when xixi is the 0/1/20/1/2-th candidate above. Transitions are straight-forward.

For multiple queries, the transitions can be written into multiplying matrices, and we can use segment tree to maintain updates.

About precision issues: actually we can avoid using floating point numbers completely. Note that all numbers in this problem are fractions with denominator Y(X+Y)Y(X+Y). Also note that the answer does not exceed (∑ai)/Y(∑ai)/Y. This means that the numerator does not exceed (∑ai)×(X+Y)<1018(∑ai)×(X+Y)<1018, so we can use long long-s to only store numerators. If you use double in C++, the relative error of one operation is less than 10−1510−15. 10−15×n<10−910−15×n<10−9, which means that using doubles is also enough.

Complexity: O(n+qlogn)O(n+qlog⁡n).

1696H - Maximum Product?

Hint 1

Find a way to calculate the maximum product that can be turned into counting.

Hint 2

Use monotonicity to reduce the complexity.

Solution

First, we describe a strategy to find the answer for a single subset. If the whole subset is negative, the answer is the product of the KK maximum numbers in it. Otherwise, take KK numbers with the maximum absolute value. If there is an even number of negative numbers in those numbers, we're done. Otherwise, find the smallest positive element and change it to the absolute-value-maximum negative element unchosen, or find the largest negative element and change it to the maximum positive element unchosen. We either do the first change or do the second change.

This gives a direct dp solution. Take all aiai and sort them into two arrays consisting of positive and negative ones (0 goes to arbitary one), pospos and negneg, sorted by absolute values. By calculating fpos(i,k)fpos(i,k): the sum of the product of the largest KK numbers of all subsets of pos[1…i]pos[1…i] that contain posiposi, and fneg(i,k)fneg(i,k) similarly, and gneg(i,k)gneg(i,k), the sum of the product of the absolute value smallest KK numbers of all subsets of neg[i…|neg|]neg[i…|neg|] that contain neginegi, and enumerating the following, we can solve the original problem in O(n5)O(n5):

  • the number of positive elements in the KK numbers with the maximum absolute value in our calculating subset, pp.
  • the smallest positive element in the KK numbers with the maximum absolute value, posiposi.
  • the greatest negative element in the KK numbers with the maximum absolute value, negjnegj.
  • (if pp is odd) the greatest positive element not in the KK numbers with the maximum absolute value, poskposk.
  • (if pp is odd) the smallest negative element not in the KK numbers with the maximum absolute value, poslposl.

The contribution to the answer can be represented as the sum of product of fpos,fnegfpos,fneg, and powers of two. I left the details as an exercise.

However, notice that the "enumerating k,lk,l" part has nothing to do with pp, so we can pre-calculate the contribution of k,lk,l for every pair (i,j)(i,j), giving an O(n4)O(n4) algorithm.

What's more, for fixed i,j,ki,j,k, the ll-s that we do the first change is a prefix/suffix of all ll, and ll-s that we do the second change is a prefix/suffix of all ll. So with two pointers and prefix sums, we can pre-calculate the contribution of every (i,j)(i,j) in O(n3)O(n3), which is fast enough.

You might be afraid that 60036003 is too slow for 1.5 seconds. However, the two O(n3)O(n3) parts of the algorithm actually run in O(n×cntpos×cntneg)O(n×cntpos×cntneg) (cntpos,cntnegcntpos,cntneg are the number of positive/negative integers in the array), leading to an at most 1/41/4 constant factor. Thus, the algorithm actually runs very fast (less than 0.25 seconds). However for similar constant factor reasons, the O(n4)O(n4) solution only takes about 6.5 seconds on Codeforces, so we had to set a seemingly-tight limit.

Tutorial of Codeforces Global Round 21

Thank you for the fast editorial feecIe6418 Congrats on your first Global Round. It's hard to imagine that you single-handedly created all except for one problems for this round, that's amazing! Great work!

Feedback

I really liked the problems. Personally I felt that the problem statements were not very lengthy (which is good) and not too hard to understand for low-rated players.

Honestly, I only checked out the first three problems, but I found them really interesting. I was able to work out the solutions for the first two, sample test cases were also good in my opinion, no problems with that.

Unfortunately due to a power cut in my locality, I didn't have internet access for the first hour of the contest and this decided not to participate. Looking forward to upsolving in virtual participation.

Also, many congratulations once again on your first Global Round. It's hard to imagine the amount of work that goes into making these problem statements and testing them. Kudos to the team and to all testers and problem setters on the platform!!

silxi

silxi
4 years ago, hide # |
 
+48

Solution for E without using the identity given in the editorial: Let DiDi be the ii'th diagonal, the set of (x,y)(x,y) such that x+y=ix+y=i. Calculate the answer by summing each diagonal. When moving from DiDi to Di+1Di+1, the current number of moves is simply multiplied by 22, except in the case where some cell in DiDi is a "boundary" (the cell down or to the right is black). But there are only O(n)O(n) such boundary cells, so we can simply subtract their contribution as we go along.

T1duS

T1duS
4 years ago, hide # |
 
+119
Easier solution for F:

Firstly, notice that if you know a single edge of the tree, you can figure out all the edges. (If you know an edge u,v first all w such that dist[u][v] = dist[u][w] and add edge u,w. Then continue this process). In the end, you can verify the existence in O(n^3). Now, realize that out of (1,2), (1,3) ... (1,n), one of the edges must be present. So, try all of these n-1 possible edges and check if you get a valid tree.

→ Reply

    123thunderbuddie
    4 years ago, show (+1) #
    »
    »
    123thunderbuddie
    4 years ago, hide # ^ |
    ← Rev. 2 →  
    +12

    For row i : the summation is iCi + (i+1)Ci + (i+2)Ci ... + (i+v[i]-1)Ci.

    Now if we know iCi = 1 = (i+1)C(i+1)

    Replacing this in the above formula we get the summation for row i as :

    (i+1)C(i+1) + (i+1)Ci + (i+2)Ci ... + (i+v[i]-1)Ci.

    Combine the first 2 terms : (i+1)C(i+1) + (i+1)Ci = (i+2)C(i+1) [ Using the simple property nCr = (n-1)C(r-1) + (n-1)Cr ]

    Replacing this in the equation we get :

    (i+2)C(i+1) + (i+2)Ci ... + (i+v[i]-1)Ci

    Again now, we can combine the next 2 terms and so on. So finally we get (i+v[i])C(i+1)

    → Reply
    • »
      »
      »
      brkdnmz
      4 years ago, show #
      »
      »
      »
      brkdnmz
      4 years ago, hide # ^ |
      ← Rev. 4 →  
      +22

      Thinking on how to come up with a visual approach, I have found the following one. It does nothing different than what 123thunderbuddie did, but supports the idea visually.

      We want to find the sum of the number of ways to reach every cell in the ithith row. Assuming there are cc cells, the sum is equal to the following (just what 123thunderbuddie wrote):

      (ii)+(i+1i)+⋯+(i+c−1i)(ii)+(i+1i)+⋯+(i+c−1i)

      Suppose there is a row below that has the same number of cells cc (not the (i+1)th(i+1)th row but a temporary one). This sum is actually equal to number of ways to reach the last cell in that row, which is (i+ci+1)(i+ci+1) (number of ways to reach cell (i+1,c−1)(i+1,c−1) from (0,0)(0,0)). Here is a visualization:

      → Reply

devo_bhai
4 years ago, show (+2) #
»
devo_bhai
4 years ago, hide # |
← Rev. 2 →  
+20

We can do D in O(N). By maintaining premax,premin,sufmax,sufmin.

Spoiler
ll n;
    cin>>n;
    vector<ll> a(n);
    cin>>a;
    vector<ll> loc(n+1);
    for(ll i=0;i<n;i++){
        loc[a[i]] = i+1;
    }
    vector<vector<ll>> adj(n+1);
    vector<ll> sufmax(n+2),sufmin(n+2);
    sufmax[n-1] = a[n-1];
    sufmin[n-1] = a[n-1];
    for(ll i=n-2;i>=0;i--){
        sufmax[i] = max(sufmax[i+1],a[i]);
        sufmin[i] = min(sufmin[i+1],a[i]);
    }
    vector<ll> premin(n+1),premax(n+1);
    premin[0] = a[0]; 
    premax[0] = a[0];
    for(ll i=1;i<n;i++){
        premin[i] = min(premin[i-1],a[i]);
        premax[i] = max(premax[i-1],a[i]);
    }
    for(ll i=0;i<n;i++){
        debug(premin[i],premax[i],sufmin[i],sufmax[i]);
        if(premin[i] < a[i] and a[i] == premax[i]){
            adj[i+1].push_back(loc[premin[i]]);
            adj[loc[premin[i]]].push_back(i+1);
        }
        if(premax[i] > a[i] and a[i] == premin[i]){
            adj[i+1].push_back(loc[premax[i]]);
            adj[loc[premax[i]]].push_back(i+1);
        }
        if(sufmax[i] > a[i] and a[i] == sufmin[i]){
            adj[i+1].push_back(loc[sufmax[i]]);
            adj[loc[sufmax[i]]].push_back(i+1);
        }
        if(sufmin[i] < a[i] and a[i] == sufmax[i]){
            adj[i+1].push_back(loc[sufmin[i]]);
            adj[loc[sufmin[i]]].push_back(i+1);
        }
    }
    debug(adj);
    auto bfs = [&](ll x){
        vector<ll> vis(n+1),dist(n+1);
        vis[x] = 1; dist[x] = 0;
        queue<ll> q;
        q.push(x);
        while(q.size()){
            auto node = q.front(); q.pop();
            for(auto child: adj[node]){
                if(vis[child] == 0){
                    vis[child] = 1;
                    dist[child] = 1 + dist[node];
                    q.push(child);
                }
            }
        }
        return dist[n];
    };
    cout << bfs(1) << endl;

    Just maintain vector of pairs where pairs.first = actual value and pair.second = frequency . Make vector of both a and b . And check at last a == b or not. Since we can divide every no m times even in the worst case it's log(n), which means atmost 30times for each element. which is still O(N). Code--

    Spoiler
    #include <bits/stdc++.h>
#include<cassert>
#include<cstring>
using namespace std;
#define mem(a, b) memset(a, (b), sizeof(a))
#define all(x) begin(x), end(x)
#define rall(x) rbegin(x), rend(x)
#define mp make_pair
#define print2d(mat,n,m){for(int i=0;i<n;i++){for(int j=0;j<m;j++){cout<<mat[i][j]<< ' ';}cout<< endl;}}
using ll = long long int; using ld = long double; using ull = unsigned long long;

//debug template
void __print(int x) {cerr << x;} void __print(long x) {cerr << x;}
void __print(long long x) {cerr << x;} void __print(unsigned x) {cerr << x;}
void __print(unsigned long x) {cerr << x;} void __print(unsigned long long x) {cerr << x;}
void __print(float x) {cerr << x;} void __print(double x) {cerr << x;}
void __print(long double x) {cerr << x;} void __print(char x) {cerr << '\'' << x << '\'';}
void __print(const char *x) {cerr << '\"' << x << '\"';} void __print(const string &x) {cerr << '\"' << x << '\"';}
void __print(bool x) {cerr << (x ? "true" : "false");}
 
//Print Template.
template<typename T, typename V>
void __print(const pair<T, V> &x) {cerr << '{'; __print(x.first); cerr << ','; __print(x.second);
    cerr << '}';}
template<typename T>
void __print(const T &x) {int f = 0; cerr << '{'; for (auto &i: x) cerr << (f++ ? "," : ""),
                __print(i); cerr << "}";}
void _print() {cerr << "]\n";}
template <typename T, typename... V>
void _print(T t, V... v) {__print(t); if (sizeof...(v)) cerr << ", "; _print(v...);}
#ifndef ONLINE_JUDGE
#define debug(x...) cerr << "[" << #x << "] = ["; _print(x)
#else
#define debug(x...)
#endif

template<typename T> istream& operator>>(istream& is,  vector<T>& v){for (auto& i : v) is >> i;return is;}
template<typename T> ostream& operator<<(ostream& os,  vector<T>& v){for (auto& i : v) os << i << ' ';return os;}

const int N = 2e5 + 100; const ll inf = 2e18; ll mod1 = 998244353; ll mod = 1e9 + 7; const int imax = 1 << 30;

// C:\mingw64\include\c++\12.1.0\bits    g++ -std=c++20 stdc++.h


void loop(){
    ll n,m;
    cin>>n>>m;
    vector<ll> a(n);
    cin>>a;
    ll k;
    cin>>k;
    vector<ll> b(k);
    cin>>b;
    auto get = [&](vector<ll> &a) -> vector<pair<ll,ll>> {
        vector<pair<ll,ll>> res;
        ll sz = a.size();
        for(ll i=0;i<sz;i++){
            pair<ll,ll> T; ll cnt = 1; ll cur = a[i];
            while(cur % m == 0 and cur){
                cnt *= m;
                cur /= m;
            }
            if(res.empty() or res.back().first != cur){
                res.push_back({cur,cnt});
            }
            else{
                res.back().second += cnt;
            }
        }
        return res;
    };  
    vector<pair<ll,ll>> A = get(a);
    vector<pair<ll,ll>> B = get(b);
    cout << (A == B ? "YES" : "NO") << "\n";
}



int main(){
    ios_base::sync_with_stdio(false);cin.tie(nullptr); cout.tie(nullptr); ll t = 1;
    cin>>t;
    for(ll i=0;i<t;i++){
        // cout << "Case #" << i+1 << ": ";
        loop();
    }
}

Another solution for F: Let S(u)S(u) be a set of pairs (x,y)(x,y) such that d(x,u)=d(y,u)d(x,u)=d(y,u). Notice that uu with minimal number of elements in S(u)S(u) is a leaf and the vertex connected to it is a vertex vv such that S(v)S(v) contains S(u)S(u) and for every pair (x,y)(x,y) such that (x,y)(x,y) belongs to S(v)S(v) and doesn't belong to S(u)S(u) either x=ux=u or y=uy=u. We can remove that leaf and continue the process.

← Rev. 3 →  
+32

I have another solution for F. It was very painful to implement, though, so I couldn't finish it during the contest. Maybe some of you had a similar thought.

First of all, if x and y are even distance apart, then there must be a valid z that's equidistant from them (center of their path). Also, if x and y are an odd distance apart, then no valid z will occur, clearly. Therefore we can create a graph where there is an edge between x and y if there is a valid such z. This graph must be bipartite, so we will be able to split vertices into 2 categories.

Now we will solve for the adjacent vertices of every vertex. After this, it will be pretty easy to construct a solution (and check if it is false) as we can just add in all the apparent edges. Consider the vertex v. Now, for all other vertices, we can form equivalence groups by their distance to v. Then, for each of these groups, we can check if every 2 vertices of that group are equidistant. If so, then clearly every one of these vertices is in a different component of the "graph without v". Now the size of the largest of those groups must be the degree of v, as each outgoing edge of v signifies one connected component on the "graph without v". Of course, we still are not able to pinpoint which one of these groups contains the vertices distance 1 around v. Let's say there are m such groups (groups where every 2 vertices are equidistant from each other and every vertex is equidistant from v with the largest possible size). Then they must be "circles" around v from distance 1 to m. We are trying to find the group of distance 1. This is where the bipartite property comes in: consider any point p from a group k distance away from v. If p and v are in different bipartite categories, then they are at an odd distance away from each other. Otherwise, they are even distanced away, and there will be exactly one single vertex z that is equidistant from them both, which will be in the middle of the path between p and v. This z will clearly be in the equivalence group with distance k/2 away from v. let's call this operation (obtaining a new vertex with k/2 distance) splitting. Now consider the equivalence group that splits the most. It is clear that if k=2bak=2ba( a odd), then b will be the times it splits. Then, it is obvious that the largest power of 2 less than or equal to k will satisfy this property. The important part is that a vertex with a power-of-2 distance will eventually split into a vertex of distance 1 from v, and so we can obtain all the other vertices of distance 1 around v, and we are done.

I believe this method as described takes O(n3logn)O(n3log⁡n) time, and it could be simplified to O(n3)O(n3)

→ Reply

    I came up with this solution too, and it unfortunately is incorrect (or perhaps incomplete).

    Then they must be "circles" around v from distance 1 to m.

    This statement is false (I had based my solution around this assumption too).

    Consider the following tree (I have highlighted the maximum size levels of vertices which have all their pairwise lca's at root (which you call "circles") with red):

    Spoiler

    So we can easily construct trees in which circles are not at one contiguous range of distance, but rather present at disjoint ranges of distance (and this totally breaks our ability to find the circle at distance 1 by halving the number of valid of levels at each step).

    Implementation of this approach: link

+15

Here is my solution to problem D:

I have a recursive function that takes two-parameter (l,r)(l,r) positions of the starting and ending index in an array and calculates the optimal answer for this range.

For the optimal answer, I first find the index of the minimum element and maximum element in the range (l,r)(l,r) and store them in ss, ee. (s<e)(s<e). I am using a segment tree and a map to find the index of the minimum and maximum in the range (l,r)(l,r). It is optimal to have an edge between (s,e)(s,e), so we add one edge.

So, The answer for the range (l,r)(l,r) is 11 + the answer for (l,s)(l,s) + the answer for (e,r)(e,r), and the base case is that if ll == rr, there is no need to add an edge, so return 00.

Solution link

https://codeforces.com/contest/1696/submission/161807339

→ Reply

+31

Here is another solution for F:

Assume that for some two nodes uu and vv, we know there exist an edge between them in the solution.

From this, we can find all vertices ww such that dist(u,v)=dist(v,w)dist(u,v)=dist(v,w), then the solution must also contain the edge (v,w)(v,w).

Repeating that process, we can find the solution (if exists) in a bfs-like way.

Therefore, for each xx from 22 to nn we can suppose that the edge (1,x)(1,x) exists in the solution, try to find that solution, and see if it is valid.

Here is O(n3)O(n3) solution to F. First we can find the farthest distance from any vertex xx to other. So we know the diameter of the tree. If we root the tree at the center, we can know the depth of every vertex. So we can determine at least one edge in the tree (node with depth 1 and the root), then use the method in the editorial. It also shows there is at most one valid answer.

+16

In problem G, when I set

const double INF = 1.0 / 0.0;

It got TLE on test 76 (or Pretest Passed 5880ms, and TLE on test 7 if I unroll the loops in matrix multiplication myself) with GNU C++14 (32bit), but ~850ms with GNU C++20 (64bit, winlib).

However, when I set

const double INF = 1e18;

Its speed remains unchanged with GNU C++20 (64bit, winlib), but when compiled by GNU C++14 (32bit), it works in <500ms and becomes the fastest submission.

Is there any logic behind this (about inf in double)?

→ Reply
  • »
    »
    Nea1
    4 years ago, show #
    »
    »
    Nea1
    4 years ago, hide # ^ |
    ← Rev. 3 →  
    0

    An explanation for the first (but not very informative) is that diving by zero (even with floating numbers) is undefined behavior, (according to [expr.mul.4], or discussions in StackOverflow), and anything can happen with undefined behavior.

    → Reply

    »
    chromate00
    4 years ago, show #
    »
    »
    chromate00
    4 years ago, hide # ^ |
    ← Rev. 2 →  
    0

    a better suggestion might be using std::numeric_limits<double>::infinity() as the value for inf. this is not undefined behaviour and still compares bigger than the maximum value representable with double.

    → Reply

+1

If you are/were getting a WA/RE verdict on problems from this contest, you can get the smallest possible counter example for your submission on cfstress.com. To do that, click on the relevant problem's link below, add your submission ID, and edit the table (or edit compressed parameters) to increase/decrease the constraints.

  • A: NIT orz!
  • B: NIT Destroys the Universe
  • C: Fishingprince Plays With Array
  • D: Permutation Graph
  • E: Placing Jinas

If you are not able to find a counter example even after changing the parameters, reply to this thread (only till the next 7 days), with links to your submission and ticket(s).

→ Reply

      • »

        benjamin_kleyn
        4 years ago, show #
        »
        »
        »
        »
        benjamin_kleyn
        4 years ago, hide # ^ |
         
        +3

        Now notice that it is possible to obtain a′a′ from aa after some operations, and it is possible to obtain bb from b′b′ after some operations, so if a′=b′a′=b′, we can obtain bb from aa.

        We still need to prove that if a′≠b′a′≠b′, it is impossible to obtain bb from aa after any number of operations.

        To do so, note that the fully expanded form of aa does not change when you perform one of the operations on it.

        → Reply

What does it mean by "z will always become a submask of itself after any number of operations, so ai will always be a submask of (ai or z) after any number of operations"? Please explain.

→ Reply
  • »
    »
    benjamin_kleyn
    4 years ago, show (+4) #
    »
    »
    benjamin_kleyn
    4 years ago, hide # ^ |
     
    0

    Basically, zz will only lose bits.

    So (ai or z′)≤(ai or z)(ai or z′)≤(ai or z), where z′z′ is zz after some operations.

      Okay, but what is the meaning of submask here specifically. Is it like changing the set bits of z after any number of operations so the resulting bit representation i.e. bit mask obtained is called submask?

      → Reply
      • »
        »
        »
        »
        benjamin_kleyn
        4 years ago, show (+2) #
        »
        »
        »
        »
        benjamin_kleyn
        4 years ago, hide # ^ |
         
        0

        z′z′ is a submask of the bitmask zz, since every bit that is set in z′z′ is also set in zz, but not necessarily vice versa.

Why this code is working fine for question A? for(int i = 0; i < a.size(); i++){ cin >> a[i]; a[i] = a[i] | z; z = a[i] & z; ans = max(ans, a[i]); } Here I am performing both the operations — bitwise or and bitwise and. Also why it is not needed to perform second operation i.e bitwise as given in tutorial, any proof?

→ Reply
  • »
    »
    LeticiaFCS
    4 years ago, show #
    »
    »
    LeticiaFCS
    4 years ago, hide # ^ |
     
    0

    You are doing:

    	x = (a[i] | z)
	y = x & z
	z = y

    Every bit in z that is 1 is also 1 in x, so (x & z) equals z. In other words, you aren't changing the value of z and the second operation is doing nothing.

    → Reply

+18

For F, for each ii, one can assume that edge (1,i)(1,i) is in the tree, and then try to construct the rest of the tree, as per hint 2. We can then check if the tree is valid.

At the start, for each node vv, we can do a O(n2α(n))O(n2α(n)) precomputation of the equivalence classes of nodes equidistant from vv. Then, we can do each tree reconstruction and check in O(n2)O(n2), for a total complexity of O(n3α(n))O(n3α(n)).

→ Reply

towrist
4 years ago, show #

towrist
4 years ago, hide # |
 
0

I got a solution for F which solves it in O(n3)O(n3)

Well-known Theorem: there is atmost two centres in a tree (a center is a vertex with minimum value of maximum distance of a vertex from the given vertex: this value is called eccentricity).

Note that using the given information, we can find the eccentricity of a vertex: this is just the number of equivalence classes of vertices wrt any given vertex (two vertices belong to the same equivalence class wrt a given vertex if they have the same distance from that given vertex).

So we can easily find the centre(s) of the tree.

If there are two centers, there is a theorem that they must be connected, so we find an edge immediately and proceed to construct the tree.

If there is one center, then we can prove that all adjacent vertices have eccentricity of exactly one greater than the center, and all other non-adjacent vertices have eccentricity ≥e+2≥e+2, where ee is eccentricity of the center. So in this case, we can find any node with e′=e+1e′=e+1 and hence, these two must be connected, and we have found an edge.

→ Reply

In problem E, if you don't know the equation : ∑ki=0Cnn+i=Cn+1n+k+1∑i=0kCn+in=Cn+k+1n+1, here's the proof:

first, you have to know Cba=Cba−1+Cb−1a−1Cab=Ca−1b+Ca−1b−1

then,

∑ki=0Cnn+i=Cnn+Cnn+1+Cnn+2+...+Cnn+k=Cn+1n+1+Cnn+1+Cnn+2...+Cnn+k=Cn+1n+2+Cnn+2+...+Cnn+k=Cn+1n+3+...+Cnn+k=...=Cn+1n+k+Cnn+k=Cn+1n+k+1∑i=0kCn+in=Cnn+Cn+1n+Cn+2n+...+Cn+kn=Cn+1n+1+Cn+1n+Cn+2n...+Cn+kn=Cn+2n+1+Cn+2n+...+Cn+kn=Cn+3n+1+...+Cn+kn=...=Cn+kn+1+Cn+kn=Cn+k+1n+1
